message:
feat(grapheme_pool): add utilization monitoring and soft limits

Enhance GraphemePool with resource management features for better memory
control in long-running applications.

New PoolStats structure:
- total_slots: All allocated slots including freed
- active_slots: Currently in-use slots
- free_slots: Available for reuse
- soft_limit: Configurable capacity advisory limit
- utilization_percent: Current usage percentage

New methods:
- with_soft_limit(): Create pool with custom soft limit
- stats(): Get current utilization metrics
- is_above_threshold(): Check if utilization is high

Constants:
- DEFAULT_SOFT_LIMIT: 1M entries default
- HIGH_UTILIZATION_THRESHOLD: 80% utilization warning level

Also includes terminal capabilities and text editor updates.
Syncs beads issue tracker.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-1ob","title":"demo_showcase runtime: resilience + capability gating implementation","description":"# demo_showcase Runtime — Resilience + Capability Gating Implementation

## Purpose

The demo must look great on ideal terminals, but it must also be robust when:

- terminal is too small
- true color is not supported
- hyperlinks are unsupported
- mouse events aren’t available
- stdout/stderr aren’t TTYs

This bead implements the degradation rules defined in `bd-1i7`.

## Behaviors

### Small Terminal

- Define a minimum supported size (from spec).
- Below that:
  - switch to a compact layout (single column or stacked panels)
  - reduce visual density (fewer borders, smaller headers)
  - ensure no panics / out-of-bounds due to rect math

### Missing Capabilities

- If no truecolor: use a reduced palette (256-color-safe or basic).
- If no hyperlinks: render link text without OSC 8 and add a `(copy)` hint.
- If no mouse: hide click hints, ensure keyboard-only navigation covers everything.
- If focus events not available: don’t depend on them for pause behavior.

### Non‑TTY

- Provide a safe fallback:
  - `--headless-smoke` should work without a TTY.
  - If run interactively without TTY and not headless, print a one-line guidance message and exit with non-zero.

## Capability Override (Testability)

To make resilience testable and debuggable, the demo supports an optional capability preset override:

- `--cap-preset <name>` (see `bd-2iv`)

Rules:

- Interactive (TTY): presets can only DISABLE capabilities (effective = detected ∩ preset).
- Headless: presets define the effective caps directly.

The UI should surface effective capabilities (inspector + status hints) so the viewer understands why a feature is disabled.

This is exercised by the degradation matrix suite (`bd-2bnv`).

## Acceptance Notes

- The demo never panics due to resize or missing terminal features.
- UI clearly indicates when features are disabled (subtle warning in status/inspector).
- Degradation behavior is consistent and snapshottable in headless mode.","acceptance_criteria":"Done when:
- The demo never panics due to missing capabilities or resize/relayout.
- Below minimum size, compact layout is activated and remains usable (help/quit always visible).
- When features are disabled (no mouse/links/truecolor/sync), the UI degrades gracefully and indicates the limitation.
- `--cap-preset` only disables capabilities in interactive mode (effective = detected ∩ preset) and defines caps in headless mode.
- The degradation matrix suite (`bd-2bnv`) passes and provides ongoing regression coverage.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"ubuntu","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:25:49.434807357Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T03:34:31.497181251Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T03:34:31.497040558Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["demo_showcase","runtime"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1ob","depends_on_id":"bd-1i7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:32:10.635614919Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1ob","depends_on_id":"bd-1xsw","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T23:32:16.340490585Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1ob","depends_on_id":"bd-29z","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:25:49.450381440Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1ob","depends_on_id":"bd-2iv","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:32:11.169561512Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1ob","depends_on_id":"bd-3ii","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:32:11.747840355Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1ob","depends_on_id":"bd-ix0","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:32:12.288427025Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1ob","depends_on_id":"bd-pnln","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:55:18.077506237Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1ok","title":"demo_showcase spec: visual design system (palette, typography, spacing, components)","description":"# demo_showcase Spec — Visual Design System

## Goal

A consistent, modern visual language that makes OpenTUI feel premium:

- strong spacing rhythm
- crisp borders
- tasteful gradients
- glassy overlays
- clear focus/selection

This spec defines **multiple themes** because the demo explicitly showcases theme switching (UI + highlighting) as a real app capability.

## Theme Set

### Theme A: “Synthwave Professional” (Default, Dark)

Base background:
- BG0 = #0f1220 (app background)
- BG1 = #151a2e (panel background)
- BG2 = #1d2440 (raised surfaces / cards)

Text:
- FG0 = #e6e6e6 (primary)
- FG1 = #aeb6d6 (secondary)
- FG2 = #6c7396 (dim)

Accents:
- Aqua   = #4dd6ff
- Pink   = #ff4fd8
- Green  = #2bff88
- Orange = #ffb020
- Red    = #ff4455

Selection + Focus:
- selection bg: #2a335c (typically applied as a low-alpha overlay)
- focus border: Aqua (and bold)

### Theme B: “Light (Paper)”

Base background:
- BG0 = #f7f7fb
- BG1 = #ffffff
- BG2 = #eef0f7

Text:
- FG0 = #1a1b26
- FG1 = #3a3f5a
- FG2 = #6a6f8a

Accents:
- Blue   = #2a6fff
- Purple = #7b61ff
- Green  = #00a86b
- Orange = #ff8a00
- Red    = #e53935

Selection + Focus:
- selection bg: #dbe6ff (low alpha)
- focus border: Blue

### Theme C: “Solarized-ish” (Low Eye Strain)

Base background:
- BG0 = #002b36
- BG1 = #073642
- BG2 = #0b4452

Text:
- FG0 = #eee8d5
- FG1 = #93a1a1
- FG2 = #657b83

Accents:
- Cyan   = #2aa198
- Yellow = #b58900
- Orange = #cb4b16
- Red    = #dc322f
- Blue   = #268bd2

Selection + Focus:
- selection bg: #0d5161 (low alpha)
- focus border: Cyan

### Theme D: “High Contrast” (Accessibility / Worst-Case Terminals)

Base background:
- BG0 = #000000
- BG1 = #000000
- BG2 = #111111

Text:
- FG0 = #ffffff
- FG1 = #e0e0e0
- FG2 = #a0a0a0

Accents:
- Cyan   = #00ffff
- Yellow = #ffff00
- Magenta= #ff00ff
- Green  = #00ff00
- Red    = #ff0000

Selection + Focus:
- selection bg: #333333
- focus border: Yellow

## Component Styling Tokens (Theme-Relative)

- Panel border: dim FG2
- Focused panel border: focus accent + bold + optional 1px “glow line”
- Headers: gradient bar + bold title
- Status bar: BG2 with muted text; hotkeys in FG0 bold
- Toasts: rounded box + opacity stack (glass)

## Typography / Characters

- Prefer Unicode box drawing (rounded/heavy where appropriate).
- Use subtle separators: │, ─, ·.
- Keep iconography mostly to single-codepoint symbols for stable width.

## Gradient Rules

- Gradients should be subtle: 5–10% perceived change across width.
- Use Rgba::lerp between two close hues.

## Theme Switching Rules

- Theme switching changes BOTH:
  1) UI palette/token set
  2) syntax highlighting theme (Theme::dark/light/…)

The demo should present theme switching as a real workflow feature:

- via command palette
- optionally via number keys when help is open (discoverable)

## Degradation Rules (Terminal Capability Variance)

- If no truecolor, map theme colors to the closest 256-color approximation.
- Avoid relying on italics/strikethrough; treat as “bonus” if supported.

## What This Bead Produces

- Canonical theme token sets used everywhere in the demo.
- A single “current theme” selection mechanism shared by chrome, panels, overlays, toasts, and highlighting.","acceptance_criteria":"Checklist:
- [ ] Palette tokens defined (BG/FG/Accents)
- [ ] Focus/selection styling rules defined
- [ ] Component styling rules defined (borders, headers, status, toasts)
- [ ] Gradient rules defined
- [ ] Degradation rules defined","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:06:10.420443553Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T00:18:02.358210255Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T00:18:02.358144072Z","close_reason":"Completed: Created docs/DEMO_SHOWCASE_DESIGN_SYSTEM.md with 4 theme definitions, component styling, typography, spacing, gradients, and degradation rules","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["demo_showcase","design","ui"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1ok","depends_on_id":"bd-2jc","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:06:10.450428345Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1ok","depends_on_id":"bd-2mu","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:07:58.107964883Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
-{"id":"bd-1pa8","title":"Fix over-permissive terminal capability defaults","description":"## Problem

src/terminal/capabilities.rs lines 59-81 defaults to very permissive capabilities:

```rust
impl Default for Capabilities {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self {
            color: ColorSupport::TrueColor,  // Assumes best case!
            unicode: true,
            mouse: true,
            focus: true,
            bracketed_paste: true,
            // ... all enabled
        }
    }
}
```

## Impact

If capability detection fails (e.g., broken TERM variable, unusual terminal):
- Code assumes TrueColor support
- Sends true color escape sequences to terminal that doesn't support them
- Terminal displays garbage or ignores sequences
- User sees corrupted output

## Better Approach

Default to minimal capabilities and only enable after confirmation:

```rust
impl Default for Capabilities {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self {
            color: ColorSupport::Basic,  // Conservative default
            unicode: false,  // Don't assume
            mouse: false,
            focus: false,
            bracketed_paste: false,
            // ... minimal defaults
        }
    }
}
```

Then in detect():
```rust
pub fn detect() -> Self {
    let mut caps = Self::default();  // Start minimal
    
    // Enable features based on detection
    if has_true_color_env() {
        caps.color = ColorSupport::TrueColor;
    }
    // ...
}
```

## Trade-off

Conservative defaults mean:
- Basic terminals work correctly out of the box
- Advanced terminals get detected features
- But if detection fails, features are disabled (not broken)

## Files to Modify

- src/terminal/capabilities.rs: Default impl, detect() method

## Testing

1. Test with missing TERM variable
2. Test with invalid TERM variable
3.  {"id":"bd-1pd","title":"demo_showcase UI: implement design system (palette + style helpers + gradients)","description":"# demo_showcase UI — Implement Design System

## Purpose

Turn the design system spec (`bd-1ok`) into code inside the demo binary:

- theme token sets (multiple themes)
- color tokens (`Rgba` constants)
- style builders (header style, border style, focus style, selection style)
- gradient helpers using `Rgba::lerp`

## Implementation Pattern

Keep it self-contained inside `src/bin/demo_showcase.rs`:

- `enum UiTheme { SynthwaveDark, PaperLight, Solarized, HighContrast }`
- `struct Tokens { bg0, bg1, bg2, fg0, fg1, fg2, accent_primary, ... }`
- `impl UiTheme { fn tokens(self, caps: &Capabilities) -> Tokens }`

No file proliferation unless truly necessary.

## Highlight Theme Mapping

Theme switching should update BOTH:

1) UI palette tokens
2) syntax highlighting theme used by the editor panel

Current highlight themes available in the library are:

- Theme::dark()
- Theme::light()
- Theme::high_contrast()

So the mapping should be:

- UiTheme::SynthwaveDark -> Theme::dark()
- UiTheme::PaperLight -> Theme::light()
- UiTheme::Solarized -> Theme::dark() (closest available unless we add a Solarized highlight theme later)
- UiTheme::HighContrast -> Theme::high_contrast()

## Must Demonstrate

- Truecolor gradients when available (still okay if approximated)
- cohesive spacing and hierarchy
- strong focus/selection affordances that remain readable in reduced-color terminals

 {"id":"bd-1qe","title":"Add Terminal Cursor Save/Restore Methods","description":"## Overview
Add Terminal methods for cursor save/restore. The ANSI sequences already exist.

## Already Implemented (NO WORK NEEDED)
In `src/ansi/sequences.rs`:
- `CURSOR_SAVE: &str = "\x1b7"` ✓
- `CURSOR_RESTORE: &str = "\x1b8"` ✓

## Scope (Methods to Add)
```rust
// In src/terminal/mod.rs
impl Terminal<W> {
    /// Save cursor position using DEC sequence.
    pub fn save_cursor(&mut self) -> io::Result<()> {
        self.write_all(CURSOR_SAVE.as_bytes())
    }

    /// Restore cursor position using DEC sequence.
    pub fn restore_cursor(&mut self) -> io::Result<()> {
        self.write_all(CURSOR_RESTORE.as_bytes())
    }
}
```

## Files to Modify
- src/terminal/mod.rs - Add the 2 methods

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests
```rust
#[test]
fn test_save_cursor_sequence() {
    let mut output = Vec::new();
    let mut terminal = Terminal::new(&mut output);
    
    terminal.save_cursor().unwrap();
    
    eprintln!("[TEST] Output bytes: {:?}", output);
    assert_eq!(output, b"\x1b7");
    
    eprintln!("[TEST] PASS: save_cursor writes correct sequence");
}

#[test]
fn test_restore_cursor_sequence() {
    let mut output = Vec::new();
    let mut terminal = Terminal::new(&mut output);
    
    terminal.restore_cursor().unwrap();
    
    eprintln!("[TEST] Output bytes: {:?}", output);
    assert_eq!(output, b"\x1b8");
    
    eprintln!("[TEST] PASS: restore_cursor writes correct sequence");
}

#[test]
fn test_save_restore_round_trip() {
    let mut output = Vec::new();
    let mut terminal = Terminal::new(&mut output);
    
    // Save, move, restore pattern
    terminal.save_cursor().unwrap();
    terminal.move_to(10, 5).unwrap();
    terminal.restore_cursor().unwrap();
    
    eprintln!("[TEST] Full sequence: {:?}", String::from_utf8_lossy(&output));
    
    // Should contain save, move, restore in order
    let s = String::from_utf8_lossy(&output);
    assert!(s.contains("\x1b7"));
    assert!(s.contains("\x1b8"));
    
    eprintln!("[TEST] PASS: save/restore round trip works");
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `save_cursor()` writes correct DEC sequence (\x1b7)
- [ ] `restore_cursor()` writes correct DEC sequence (\x1b8)
- [ ] Methods return io::Result for error handling
- [ ] All 3 unit tests pass with logging
- [ ] Existing Terminal tests still pass (no regression)","acceptance_criteria":"- [ ] Sequences are correct

 {"id":"bd-26vw","title":"EPIC: Comprehensive Test Coverage & E2E Infrastructure","description":"# EPIC: Comprehensive Test Coverage & E2E Infrastructure

Master epic for achieving full unit test coverage without mocks and complete E2E integration test scripts with detailed logging.

## Background & Rationale

OpenTUI is a high-performance terminal UI rendering engine with strict correctness requirements. The codebase needs comprehensive testing to ensure:
1. Alpha blending correctness (Porter-Duff compositing)
2. Unicode/grapheme handling accuracy
3. ANSI sequence generation correctness
4. Diff rendering efficiency
5. Hit grid precision

Testing without mocks ensures we test REAL behavior, not simulated behavior.

## Goals

1. **Line Coverage ≥90%** across all modules
2. **Real tests, no mocks** - test actual implementations
3. **Structured E2E logging** - JSONL format, CI-parseable
4. **CI-runnable** - all tests pass reliably in GitHub Actions
5. **Property-based testing** - catch edge cases automatically
6. **Visual regression detection** - golden file comparisons

## Child EPICs

### bd-tv2h: Unit Test Coverage Infrastructure
- Coverage tooling setup (cargo-llvm-cov)
- Coverage thresholds and CI gates
- Test fixture library

### bd-3k9i: demo_showcase Unit Test Expansion  
- Tests for new features (scroll offset, bounded logs, metrics)
- Headless check function tests

### bd-4cr6: E2E Integration Test Scripts
- Structured logging harness
- PTY test CI integration
- Golden file verification
- Input simulation

## Critical Path

```
                    bd-397z (Logging)
                         │
                    bd-1ci7 (Artifacts)
                         │
   bd-2azg ───┬─── bd-1z1r (PTY CI) ───── bd-3le2 (PTY Stable)
   (Fixtures) │         │                      │
              │         └────────┬─────────────┘
              │                  │
              └───── bd-35mn (Full CI/CD)
                         │
                    bd-1rov (Golden Files)
```

## Success Criteria

- [ ] cargo tarpaulin/llvm-cov reports ≥90% coverage
- [ ] All E2E tests pass in CI environment  
- [ ] Test logs are structured JSONL, queryable with jq
- [ ] No mocks or fakes in test code
- [ ] Property tests run 1000+ cases per property
- [ ] Golden file comparisons detect regressions
- [ ] PTY tests run reliably (<1% flake rate)

## Module Coverage Targets

| Module | Target | Priority |
|--------|--------|----------|
| src/color.rs | ≥95% | P0 (core algorithm) |
| src/buffer/ | ≥90% | P0 (critical path) |
| src/renderer/ | ≥85% | P0 (core feature) |
| src/ansi/ | ≥90% | P0 (terminal output) |
| src/text/ | ≥85% | P1 |
| src/input/ | ≥90% | P1 |
| src/terminal/ | ≥75% | P2 (FFI limits) |

## Testing Philosophy

1. **Test real implementations**: No MockBuffer, no FakeTerminal
2. **Capture output for verification**: MockTerminal captures ANSI sequences
3. **Property-based over example-based**: Find edge cases automatically
4. **Visual verification**: Golden files catch rendering regressions
5. **CI-first design**: All tests must work in headless CI

## Open Questions (Resolved)

- Q: How to test terminal output without real terminal?

- [ ] Malformed sequences: never panic, graceful degradation
- [ ] Key events: modifier combinations valid

-{"id":"bd-28gx","title":"Add GraphemePool size limits and utilization warnings","description":"## Problem

src/grapheme_pool.rs has no safeguards against unbounded growth:

```rust
pub struct GraphemePool {
    slots: Vec<Slot>,  // No size limit!
    free_list: Vec<u32>,
}
```

## Impact

Long-running applications can exhaust memory:
1. Application processes many unique graphemes
2. Graphemes aren't properly decref()'d (leak)
3. Pool grows indefinitely
4. Eventually OOM

## Required Features

### Size Limit
```rust
const DEFAULT_MAX_POOL_SIZE: usize = 1_000_000;  // 1M entries

pub struct GraphemePool {
    slots: Vec<Slot>,
    free_list: Vec<u32>,
    max_size: usize,  // Configurable limit
}

impl GraphemePool {
    pub fn with_max_size(max_size: usize) -> Self { ... }
    
    pub fn alloc(&mut self, grapheme: &str) -> Result<GraphemeId, PoolError> {
        if self.slots.len() >= self.max_size && self.free_list.is_empty() {
            return Err(PoolError::PoolFull);
        }
        // ...
    }
}
```

### Utilization Metrics
```rust
impl GraphemePool {
    pub fn utilization(&self) -> PoolStats {
        PoolStats {
            total_slots: self.slots.len(),
            active_slots: self.slots.len() - self.free_list.len(),
            free_slots: self.free_list.len(),
            max_size: self.max_size,
        }
    }
    
    pub fn is_high_utilization(&self) -> bool {
        let active = self.slots.len() - self.free_list.len();
        active > self.max_size * 80 / 100  // 80% threshold
    }
}
```

### Usage Warnings

Consider logging warnings at 80%, 90%, 95% utilization:
```rust
pub fn alloc(&mut self, grapheme: &str) -> GraphemeId {
    let utilization = self.active_count() * 100 / self.max_size;
    if utilization >= 95 && !self.warned_95 {
        log::warn!("GraphemePool at 95% capacity");
        self.warned_95 = true;
    }
    // ...
}
```

## Files to Modify

- src/grapheme_pool.rs: Add max_size, utilization metrics, warnings

## Testing

1. Test pool at capacity
2. Test utilization calculation
3. Verify warnings trigger at thresholds","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-01-28T23:08:46.261603815Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T23:08:46.270215047Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-28gx","depends_on_id":"bd-l4ig","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-28T23:08:46.270145116Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-2mhy","title":"EPIC: Security Audit - Escape Sequence Injection Prevention","description":"## Overview

This epic addresses a **CRITICAL SECURITY VULNERABILITY** discovered during the deep codebase audit.

## The Vulnerability

**Location:** src/terminal/mod.rs lines 258-277

The set_title() function filters control characters using char::is_control(), which only catches ASCII control characters (0x00-0x1F, 0x7F). However, it FAILS to filter C1 control characters (0x80-0x9F).

### Why This Matters

C1 control characters include:
- CSI (0x9B) - Control Sequence Introducer (equivalent to ESC [)
- OSC (0x9D) - Operating System Command (equivalent to ESC ])
- DCS (0x90) - Device Control String

An attacker who can control the window title could inject these bytes to break out of OSC sequences and inject arbitrary terminal commands.

## Fix Strategy

1. Expand filter to include C1 control bytes (0x80-0x9F)
2. Use explicit whitelist approach instead of blacklist
3. Add regression tests with malicious payloads

## Acceptance Criteria

- C1 bytes (0x80-0x9F) filtered from title strings
- Unit tests verify malicious sequences blocked
- Other OSC-emitting functions audited","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-28T23:01:52.261442580Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T23:09:38.467986882Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T23:09:38.467923675Z","close_reason":"done","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"bd-2mu","title":"demo_showcase spec: app concept + panel layout wireframe","description":"# demo_showcase Spec — App Concept + Panel Layout Wireframe

## App Concept (The “Realistic” Story)

The demo app presents itself as a **Developer Workbench**:

- You’re looking at a project
- You’re editing a file
- You have a live preview/inspector
- You have logs/notifications and quick actions

This is believable, and it naturally uses all OpenTUI primitives.

## Core Screen Layout (Baseline)

Single-screen layout with persistent chrome:

- **Top bar**: brand, mode, time, quick actions (clickable)
- **Sidebar** (left): navigation list (scrollable + clipped)
- **Main editor** (center): `EditorView` (highlighting, selection)
- **Preview** (right): pixel/charts + “compositing” demos
- **Bottom status bar**: key hints + stats
- **Toast area**: transient notifications

### ASCII Wireframe

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  OpenTUI Showcase      Project: opentui_rust      Mode: Normal     22:05   │
├───────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────┤
│ Sidebar       │ Editor                                    │ Preview       │
│ (scroll)      │ (rope + undo + highlight)                 │ (pixels)      │
│               │                                           │ (charts)      │
│  • Overview   │  fn main() {                              │   ███░░       │
│  • Editor     │      println!("hello");                   │   ▜▛▌▐        │
│  • Preview    │  }                                        │               │
│  • Logs       │                                           │  Alpha blend  │
│  • Unicode    │                                           │  Scissor clip │
│               │                                           │               │
├───────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────┴───────────────┤
│  F1 Help  Ctrl+P Command  Ctrl+T Tour  Ctrl+Q Quit   FPS:60  Cells:123     │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Navigation Model (Sidebar “Sections”)

The sidebar is not just focus; it selects a **Section** that drives what content is emphasized.

Suggested model:

- `enum Section { Overview, Editor, Preview, Logs, Unicode, Performance, Settings }`

Rules:

- In the normal (wide) layout, the main panels remain visible, but the selected section:
  - sets a sensible default focus
  - chooses what the preview area shows (pixels vs unicode showpiece vs perf graphs)
  - can change the status bar hints and the inspector emphasis

Example mapping (initial):

- Overview: default "wow" composition (pixels + logs alive + editor visible)
- Unicode: preview area becomes the grapheme pool showpiece + width rulers
- Performance: preview area becomes perf charts; inspector is pinned/expanded
- Settings: opens a settings overlay or uses the command palette as the primary UI

In compact layout (small terminals), the selected section chooses which panel gets the full-screen treatment.

## Overlay Screens

Overlays (semi-transparent, “glass”):

- **Command palette** (Ctrl+P): fuzzy-ish list + preview
- **Help overlay** (F1): keybinds + feature legend
- **Tour overlay** (Ctrl+T): step title + next action

Overlays should use:
- `push_opacity()` + blended background rectangles
- scissor clipping for internal list scroll

## Layout Rules

- Everything is manual rectangles (OpenTUI is not a layout engine).
- Each panel computes a `Rect { x,y,w,h }`.
- Panels are clipped to their rect via scissor stack.
- Borders/headers are drawn with box styles + subtle gradients.

## What This Bead Produces

- A stable “screen map” other beads can implement against.
- A clear definition of Sections vs Focus, so navigation and tour scripts remain coherent.","acceptance_criteria":"Checklist:

- [ ] Each region has explicit responsibilities + feature coverage

 {"id":"bd-61g0","title":"demo_showcase e2e: PTY harness + ANSI capture (real terminal sequences, ignored by default)","description":"# demo_showcase E2E — PTY Harness + ANSI Capture (Ignored by Default)

## Purpose

Headless tests prove the render pipeline is correct, but they cannot prove real terminal I/O behavior.

This bead adds true end-to-end tests by running the demo under a pseudo-terminal (PTY), capturing ANSI output bytes, and asserting on key invariants:

- alternate screen entry/exit
- cursor hide/show
- mouse enable sequences (if enabled)
- synchronized output enable (if supported)
- OSC 8 hyperlink sequences appear (and do NOT appear when disabled)

## Environment Normalization (Critical for Determinism)

Capability detection in OpenTUI is environment-driven. PTY runs often have sparse env.

To make E2E behavior deterministic, the PTY harness MUST set a known-good env baseline for the child process, e.g.:

- `TERM=xterm-kitty` (or another known terminal string)
- `TERM_PROGRAM=kitty` (optional if TERM-based detection is implemented, but keep for redundancy)
- `COLORTERM=truecolor`
- `LANG=C.UTF-8`

This ensures:

- `Capabilities::detect()` can enable sync output + hyperlinks on a "known" terminal (see `bd-1xsw`).

## Approach (Unix)

Use libc openpty / fork+exec (or equivalent) to spawn the demo under a PTY and capture output.

Recommended primary case:

- cargo run --all-features --bin demo_showcase -- --tour --exit-after-tour --max-frames 600 --fps 30

Why both exit controls?

- `--exit-after-tour` is the intended deterministic terminator.
- `--max-frames` is a hard safety bound so the PTY test cannot hang if tour logic regresses.

## Assertions (Positive)

Assert the output stream contains, in some order:

- enter alt screen (CSI ? 1049 h) when alt screen enabled
- leave alt screen (CSI ? 1049 l) on exit
- hide cursor (CSI ? 25 l) and show cursor (CSI ? 25 h)
- mouse enable sequences (1000/1002/1006) when mouse enabled
- synchronized output enable (CSI ? 2026 h) when supported/enabled

Hyperlink coverage:

- run one case with default settings and assert at least one OSC 8 sequence appears (ESC ] 8 ; ; ...)
- run one case with `--cap-preset no_hyperlinks` (disable) and assert OSC 8 sequences are absent

## Assertions (Negative / Flag Coverage)

Add explicit "feature disabled" cases so the CLI flags are verifiably correct:

- `--no-alt-screen`: assert alt-screen enter/exit sequences are absent
- `--no-mouse`: assert mouse enable sequences are absent

(Other invariants like cursor restore should still hold.)

## Optional (High-Value): Threaded Mode Parity

- run an additional spawn with `--threaded` and assert the same terminal lifecycle invariants

## Logging / Artifacts

When the test runs, log (to stdout/stderr, and to artifacts when enabled):

- full spawn command (argv)
- exit status + duration
- bytes captured
- TERM/TERM_PROGRAM/COLORTERM/LANG used for the child (and any env overrides)

Artifacts (controlled via HARNESS_ARTIFACTS=1):

- captured ANSI bytes (raw)
- escaped/hex view for grep-friendly debugging
- stdout/stderr from the child process

Reuse the existing harness conventions:

- HARNESS_ARTIFACTS=1
- HARNESS_ARTIFACTS_DIR=target/test-artifacts

## Test Placement

- Mark PTY tests as #[ignore] by default to avoid CI flakiness.
- Provide a script (separate bead) to run them locally and collect artifacts.","acceptance_criteria":"Done when:

- The PTY harness normalizes the child env (TERM/TERM_PROGRAM/COLORTERM/LANG) so capability detection is deterministic.
- Assertions cover: alt screen enter/exit, cursor hide/show, mouse enable (when enabled), and sync output enable (when supported/enabled).
- Hyperlink behavior is covered: OSC 8 sequences present by default and absent under `--cap-preset no_hyperlinks`.
- Flag coverage includes negative cases:
  - `--no-alt-screen` -> no 1049 enter/exit sequences
  - `--no-mouse` -> no mouse enable sequences
- Optional: a threaded run case (`--threaded`) also passes the same terminal lifecycle assertions.

-{"id":"bd-96yk","title":"Optimize GraphemePool intern() with HashMap (O(n) → O(1))","description":"## Problem

src/grapheme_pool.rs lines 166-183 uses linear search for interning:

```rust
pub fn intern(&mut self, grapheme: &str) -> GraphemeId {
    let existing = self
        .slots
        .iter()                           // O(n) - scans ALL slots
        .enumerate()
        .skip(1)
        .find(|(_, slot)| !slot.is_free() && slot.bytes == grapheme)  // String compare!
        .map(|(i, slot)| (i as u32, slot.width));
    // ...
}
```

## Performance Impact

For n unique graphemes:
- Each intern() call: O(n) scan + O(m) string compare per slot
- Rendering text with k graphemes: O(k * n) total
- With 10K unique emoji in a log viewer: ~100M operations per render

## Required Fix

Add HashMap for O(1) lookup:

```rust
pub struct GraphemePool {
    slots: Vec<Slot>,
    free_list: Vec<u32>,
    // NEW: O(1) lookup by grapheme string
    index: HashMap<String, u32>,  // grapheme → slot_id
}

pub fn intern(&mut self, grapheme: &str) -> GraphemeId {
    // O(1) lookup
    if let Some(&slot_id) = self.index.get(grapheme) {
        let slot = &mut self.slots[slot_id as usize];
        if !slot.is_free() {
            slot.refcount += 1;
            return GraphemeId::new(slot_id, slot.width);
        }
    }
    
    // Not found - allocate new
    let id = self.alloc(grapheme);
    self.index.insert(grapheme.to_owned(), id.pool_id());
    id
}

pub fn decref(&mut self, id: GraphemeId) {
    // ... existing logic ...
    if slot.refcount == 0 {
        // Remove from index when freed
        self.index.remove(&slot.bytes);
        self.free_list.push(pool_id);
    }
}
```

## Trade-offs

- Memory: +~40 bytes per unique grapheme (HashMap entry overhead)
- Complexity: Slightly more code to maintain index consistency
- Benefit: O(1) intern instead of O(n)

## Files to Modify

- src/grapheme_pool.rs: Add index field, update intern(), decref(), alloc()

## Testing

1. Benchmark intern() with 1K, 10K, 100K unique graphemes
2. Verify correctness: intern same grapheme multiple times
3. Verify cleanup: decref removes from index
4.  {"id":"bd-affo","title":"EPIC: Color System Correctness","description":"## Overview

This epic addresses **COLOR ACCURACY BUGS** discovered during the deep codebase audit. The color system has multiple issues that cause incorrect rendering:

1. **Grayscale ramp calculation** - Wrong formula for 256-color mapping
2. **Display vs to_rgb_u8 inconsistency** - Same color formats differently
3. **Missing gamma correction** - All blending done in sRGB space (wrong)
4. **Luminance calculation** - Ignores gamma, produces wrong perceived brightness

## Impact

These bugs cause:
- Colors appear different than expected in 256-color mode
- Inconsistent hex string output
- Washed-out colors when blending/averaging
- Incorrect contrast calculations for accessibility

## Background: Why Gamma Matters

sRGB is NOT linear. A value of 0.5 in sRGB represents ~0.214 in linear light.

Correct color operations require:
1. Convert sRGB → linear (apply gamma ~2.2)
2. Perform operation (blend, average, luminance)
3. Convert linear → sRGB (apply inverse gamma)

The current code skips steps 1 and 3, treating sRGB as linear.

## Scope

This epic covers:
- 256-color palette mapping fixes
- Display/format consistency
- Optional: Gamma-correct blending (significant change)

 {"id":"bd-bh0m","title":"Unit Tests: Property-Based Testing with Proptest","description":"# Property-Based Testing with Proptest (Consolidated)

Consolidated from bd-bh0m and bd-271d.

## Goal
Add property-based tests for algorithms where properties are well-defined. Target 1000+ test cases per property for thorough coverage.

## Color Properties (5 properties)

### Blending Properties
```rust
// Blending with transparent = identity
proptest! {
    fn blend_with_transparent_is_identity(c in rgba_strategy()) {
        prop_assert_eq!(c.blend_over(Rgba::TRANSPARENT), c);
    }
}

// Blending with opaque = replacement
proptest! {
    fn blend_with_opaque_replaces(a in rgba_strategy(), b in opaque_rgba_strategy()) {
        prop_assert_eq!(a.blend_over(b), b);
    }
}
```

### HSV Conversion
```rust
// RGB -> HSV -> RGB roundtrip
proptest! {
    fn hsv_roundtrip(c in rgb_strategy()) {
        let hsv = c.to_hsv();
        let back = Rgba::from_hsv(hsv.0, hsv.1, hsv.2);
        // Allow small rounding error
        prop_assert!((c.r as i32 - back.r as i32).abs() <= 1);
    }
}
```

### Color Interpolation
```rust
// Lerp bounds preserved
proptest! {
    fn lerp_preserves_bounds(a in rgba_strategy(), b in rgba_strategy(), t in 0.0f32..=1.0) {
        let result = a.lerp(b, t);
        prop_assert!(result.r <= 255 && result.g <= 255 && result.b <= 255 && result.a <= 255);
    }
}
```

## Unicode Properties (4 properties)

```rust
// Width is always non-negative

proptest! {
    fn width_is_additive(a in "\\PC*", b in "\\PC*") {

    }
}

proptest! {
```

src/text/editor.rs lines 705-728 calculates visual column incorrectly:

```rust
fn visual_col_in_vline(&self, vline: &VirtualLine, char_offset: usize) -> usize {
    let char_start = rope.byte_to_char(vline.byte_start);
    let char_end = rope.byte_to_char(vline.byte_end).min(char_offset);  // CLAMPS TO CURSOR!
    let line = rope.slice(char_start..char_end).to_string();
    // Calculates width of text UP TO cursor, not full line width
}
```

## Impact

When navigating up/down in wrapped text:
1. Cursor is at column 50 in a long line
2. User presses Up arrow
3. move_up_visual() calculates visual_col from current position
4. But it calculates width TO cursor, not full width
5. Cursor lands at wrong column in previous line

## Example

```
Line 1: |This is a very lo|ng line that wra|ps across multip|le visual lines|
        ^cursor here (col 50)

User presses Up:
- visual_col_in_vline calculates width of "This is a very lo" = 17
- Lands at column 17 of previous line instead of column 50
```

## Required Fix

Calculate visual column relative to the virtual line start, not the cursor:

```rust
fn visual_col_in_vline(&self, vline: &VirtualLine, char_offset: usize) -> usize {
    let rope = self.buffer.rope();
    let vline_char_start = rope.byte_to_char(vline.byte_start);
    
    // Calculate width from vline start TO cursor
    let cursor_in_vline = char_offset.saturating_sub(vline_char_start);
    let vline_text = rope.slice(vline_char_start..char_offset).to_string();
    
    display_width(&vline_text)
}
```

## Files to Modify

3. Navigate up/down
4. ```

### AnsiSequence Enum
```rust
pub enum AnsiSequence {
    CursorMove { row: u32, col: u32 },
    CursorRelative { dx: i32, dy: i32 },
    SetFgColor(Rgba),
    SetBgColor(Rgba),
    SetAttribute(TextAttributes),
    Reset,
    Text(String),
    Hyperlink { id: u32, url: String },
    Custom(Vec<u8>),
}
```

### Usage in Tests
```rust
#[test]
fn test_renderer_output() {
    let mock = MockTerminal::new(80, 24);

    renderer.present()?;
    
    let sequences = renderer.writer().parsed_sequences();
    assert!(sequences.contains(&AnsiSequence::SetFgColor(Rgba::RED)));
    assert!(sequences.iter().any(|s| matches!(s, AnsiSequence::Text(t) if t == "Hello")));
}
```
